title: nth term test
description: Practice using the nth term test to determine sequence divergence.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }\frac{(n+1)!}{n!}$

**True or false?**

According to the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test, the series diverges.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The $~n$th term test is only conclusive when $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}\ne 0\,$.

Since $\displaystyle~ \underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{(n+1)!}{n!}=\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,(n+1)\ne0\,$, it is correct to say that the series diverges by the $~n$th term test.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }\frac{n!}{(n+1)!}$

**True or false?**

According to the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test, the series converges.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The $~n$th term test is only conclusive when $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}\ne 0\,$.
Since $\displaystyle ~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{n!}{(n+1)!}=\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{1}{n+1}=0\,$, the test is inconclusive for this series.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}}$

**True or false?**

According to the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test, the series diverges.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Since $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}\ne 0\,$, it is correct to say that the $~n$th term test tells that $~\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}}~$ diverges.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}}{n}}$

**True or false?**

According to the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test, the series converges.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The $~n$th term test is only conclusive when $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}\ne 0\,$.

Since $\displaystyle~ \underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}}{n}=0\,$, the $~n$th term test cannot help with us determine whether $\displaystyle~~\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}}{n}}~~$ converges or diverges.

$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{n^2-1}{2n^3+1}}$

According to the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test, the series converges.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The $~n$th term test is only conclusive when $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}\ne 0\,$.
Since $\displaystyle~ \underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{n^2-1}{2n^3+1}=0\,$, the test is inconclusive for this series.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}}$

**True or false?**

According to the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test, the series diverges.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Since $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\ne 0\,$, it is correct to say that the $~n$th term test tells that $~\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}}~$ diverges.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{(-1)^n}{n!}}$

**True or false?**

According to the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test, the series converges.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The $~n$th term test is only conclusive when $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}\ne 0\,$.

Since $\displaystyle~ \underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}}{n!}=0\,$, the $~n$th term test cannot help with us determine whether $\displaystyle~\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}}{n!}}~$ converges or diverges.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{(2n+3)(n+6)}{{(n+4)(n+1)^2}}}$

**What conclusion can be reached by using the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test on the series?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The $~n$th term test says the following:

If $\displaystyle ~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}\ne 0\,$, $~~~$ then $~~~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{{a}_{n}}}~$ diverges.

If $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}=0\,$, $~~~$ then $~~~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{{a}_{n}}}~$ might converge or might diverge; i.e., the test is inconclusive.

We see that   $\displaystyle~~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{(2n+3)(n+6)}{{(n+4)(n+1)^2}}=0~~$   because the degree of the denominator is greater than the degree of the numerator. 

Therefore, the test is inconclusive.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=2}^{\infty }{\frac{(n-1)^2(n+3)}{{n^3-1}}}$

**What conclusion can be reached by using the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test on the series?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The $~n$th term test says the following:

If $\displaystyle~ \underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}\ne 0\,$, $~~~$ then $~~~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{{a}_{n}}}~$ diverges.

If $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}=0\,$, $~~~$ then $~~~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{{a}_{n}}}~$ might converge or might diverge; i.e., the test is inconclusive.

We see that   $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{(n-1)^2(n+3)}{{n^3-1}}=1~$  because the degree of the denominator is the same as the degree of the numerator. 

Therefore, the series diverges.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{n}{{n^2+1}}}$

**What conclusion can be reached by using the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test on the series?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The $~n$th term test says the following:

If $\displaystyle ~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}\ne 0\,$, $~~~$ then $~~~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{{a}_{n}}}~$ diverges.

If $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}=0\,$, $~~~$ then $~~~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{{a}_{n}}}~$ might converge or might diverge; i.e., the test is inconclusive.

We see that   $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{n}{{{n^2+1}}}=0~$  because the degree of the denominator is higher than the degree of the numerator. 

Therefore, the $~n$th term test is inconclusive.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{\sqrt{n}}{{{(n+1)}^{3}}}}$

**What conclusion can be reached by using the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test on the series?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The nth term test says the following:

If $\displaystyle~ \underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}\ne 0\,$, then $~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{{a}_{n}}}~$ diverges.

If $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}=0\,$, then $~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{{a}_{n}}}~$ might converge or might diverge; i.e., the test is inconclusive.

We see that   $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{\sqrt{n}}{{{(n+1)}^{3}}}=0~$  because the degree of the denominator is higher than the degree of the numerator. 

Therefore, the $~n$th term test is inconclusive.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{(3-n)(n^2-16)^2}{{(n+1)(5n^2-6)^3}}}$

**What conclusion can be reached by using the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test on the series?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The $~n$th term test says the following:

If $\displaystyle ~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}\ne 0\,$, $~~~$ then $~~~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{{a}_{n}}}~$ diverges.

If $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}=0\,$, $~~~$ then $~~~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{{a}_{n}}}~$ might converge or might diverge; i.e., the test is inconclusive.

We see that   $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{\frac{(3-n)(n^2-16)^2}{{(n+1)(5n^2-6)^3}}}=0~$  because the degree of the denominator is greater than the degree of the numerator. 

Therefore, the $~n$th term test is inconclusive.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=2}^{\infty }{\frac{\sqrt{n}}{{n^2-1}}}$

**What conclusion can be reached by using the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test on the series?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The $~n$th term test says the following:

If $\displaystyle ~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}\ne 0\,$, $~~~$ then $~~~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{{a}_{n}}}~$ diverges.

If $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}=0\,$, $~~~$ then $~~~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{{a}_{n}}}$ might converge or might diverge; i.e., the test is inconclusive.

We see that   $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{\sqrt{n}}{{{n^2-1}}}=0~$  because the degree of the denominator is higher than the degree of the numerator. 

Therefore, the $~n$th term test is inconclusive.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{(n-5)(4n+7)^8}{{12n^2(3n-1)}}}$

**What conclusion can be reached by using the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test on the series?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The $~n$th term test says the following:

If $\displaystyle ~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}\ne 0\,$, $~~~$ then $~~~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{{a}_{n}}}~$ diverges.

If $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}=0\,$, $~~~$ then $~~~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{{a}_{n}}}~$ might converge or might diverge; i.e., the test is inconclusive.

We see that   $\displaystyle~~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{\frac{(n-5)(4n+7)^8}{{12n^2(3n-1)}}}~~$  is unbounded  because the degree of the denominator is less than the degree of the numerator. 

Therefore, the series diverges.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{(2n-3)(n^8+1)^2}{{(6-5n)^2(4-3n^2)^2}}}$

**What conclusion can be reached by using the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test on the series?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The $~n$th term test says the following:

If $\displaystyle ~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}\ne 0\,$, $~~~$ then $~~~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{{a}_{n}}}~$ diverges.

If $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}=0\,$, $~~~$ then $~~~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{{a}_{n}}}~$ might converge or might diverge; i.e., the test is inconclusive.

We see that   $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{(2n-3)(n^8+1)^2}{{(6-5n)^2(4-3n^2)^2}}~$  is unbounded  because the degree of the denominator is less than the degree of the numerator. 

Therefore, the series diverges.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{(2n)!}{2^{2n}}=?$

[[☃ radio 1]] 

**Based on your answer, what does the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test say about $S$?**

$S=\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }\frac{(2n)!}{2^{2n}}$

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
$(2n)!~$ grows faster than $~2^{2n}\,$. To see this is true, imagine letting $~n=5,000,000\,$. Then, $~(2n)!~$ will have $~10,000,000~$ factors, almost all of which are greater than $~2\,$, while $~2^{2n}~$ has $~10,000,000~$ factors of $~2\,$.

Hence, we know  $\qquad\displaystyle\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{(2n)!}{{2^{2n}}}=\infty\,$.
Since $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}\ne 0\,$, we know by the $~n$th term test that $\displaystyle~~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{\frac{(2n)!}{{2^{2n}}}}~$ diverges.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{3^n}{{{2}^{n}}}=?$

[[☃ radio 1]] 

**Based on your answer, what does the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test say about $S$?**

$S=\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{\frac{3^n}{{{2}^{n}}}}$ 

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
$\qquad\displaystyle\frac{3^n}{2^n}=\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^n$

Hence, we know  $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{3^n}{{{2}^{n}}}=\infty\,$.
Since $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}\ne 0\,$, we know by the $~n$th term test that $\displaystyle~~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{\frac{3^n}{{{2}^{n}}}}~$ diverges.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{n!}{{{1000}^{n}}}=?$

[[☃ radio 1]] 

**Based on your answer, what does the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test say about $S$?**

$S=\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{\frac{n!}{{{1000}^{n}}}}$ 

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
$n!~$ grows faster than $~1000^n\,$.  To see that this is true, imagine letting $~n=1,000,000,000\,$. $~~n!~$ will have a significant number of factors that are much greater than 1000,  whereas $~1000^n~$ only has factors of $~1000\,$.

Hence, we know  $\qquad\displaystyle\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{n!}{{{1000}^{n}}}=\infty\,$.
Since $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}\ne 0\,$, we know by the $~n$th term test that $\displaystyle~~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{\frac{n!}{{{1000}^{n}}}}~$ diverges.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}{\frac{6n^4+4n^2-n-2}{n^5-3n^4+n^3-10n^2+6}}=?$

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Based on your answer, what does the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test say about $S$?**

$\displaystyle S=\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{\frac{6n^4+4n^2-n-2}{n^5-3n^4+n^3-10n^2+6}}$

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Remember that the nth term test is conducted as follows.

If $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}=0\,$,  then the series MIGHT converge.

$\qquad\qquad$**BUT**

If $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}~$ is anything other than zero, then the series diverges.

$\qquad\displaystyle\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{\frac{6n^4+4n^2-n-2}{n^5-3n^4+n^3-10n^2+6}}=0 $ 

because the degree of the numerator is less than the degree of the denominator.  Hence the $~n$th term test is inconclusive.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{4n^2+8n+3}{2n^2-5}=?$

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Based on your answer, what does the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test say about $S$?**

$\displaystyle S=\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{\frac{4n^2+8n+3}{2n^2-5}}$

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Remember that the $~n$th term test is conducted as follows.

If $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}=0\,$, then the series MIGHT converge.

$\qquad\qquad$**BUT**

If $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}~$ is anything other than zero, then the series diverges.

$\qquad\displaystyle\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{4n^2+8n+3}{2n^2-5}=2 $ 

because the degree of the numerator is the same as the degree of the denominator and the ratio of the leading coefficients is 2.  Hence, the series $~S~$ diverges.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{5{{n}^{3}}+17{{n}^{2}}-23n+7}{3{{n}^{2}}-14n+89}=?$

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Based on your answer, what does the $n^{\text{th}}$ term test say about $S$?**

$\displaystyle S=\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{\frac{5{{n}^{3}}+17{{n}^{2}}-23n+7}{3{{n}^{2}}-14n+89}}\,$

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Remember that the $~n$th term test is conducted as follows.

If $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}=0\,$, then the series MIGHT converge.

$\qquad\qquad$**BUT**

If $\displaystyle~\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,{{a}_{n}}~$ is anything other than zero, the series diverges.

$\displaystyle\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{5{{n}^{3}}+17{{n}^{2}}-23n+7}{3{{n}^{2}}-14n+89}=\infty~~ $ because the degree of the numerator is greater than the degree of the denominator.  

Hence the series $~S~$ diverges.
